data.title: Insmed (INSM) Is Down 10.5% After Halting Brensocatib in HS Trial Miss - What's Changed
data.description:
• Insmed Incorporated recently reported that its Phase 2b CEDAR trial of brensocatib in adults with moderate to severe hidradenitis suppurativa failed to meet primary and secondary efficacy endpoints, leading the company to halt development of the drug for this indication despite a clean safety profile.
  • An interesting aspect of the CEDAR data is that patients on placebo showed a greater reduction in abscess and inflammatory nodule counts than those on either brensocatib dose, raising questions about trial design, disease variability and future development priorities for the asset.
  • We will now examine how discontinuing brensocatib in hidradenitis suppurativa may influence Insmed’s broader brensocatib-centered investment narrative and expectations.

Insmed Investment Narrative Recap

To own Insmed, you need to believe that brensocatib in bronchiectasis and ARIKAYCE expansion can offset current losses and justify today’s valuation. The CEDAR setback in hidradenitis suppurativa appears immaterial to the near term brensocatib bronchiectasis launch catalyst, but it does highlight development risk around broader brensocatib indications.

In that context, the recent positive Phase 3b ENCORE results for ARIKAYCE, supporting a potential label expansion with an sNDA planned for H2 2026, stand out as especially relevant, as they reinforce Insmed’s respiratory focus while brensocatib’s HS program is discontinued.

Yet, while the HS decision may not change the headline catalyst, it underscores a risk investors should be aware of around...

data.title: StoneCo Special Dividend And Incentive Shares Put Capital Choices In Focus
data.description:
• StoneCo (NasdaqGS:STNE) has declared an extraordinary cash dividend tied to proceeds from the sale of Linx.
  • The company has also approved an increase in the number of shares available under its Long Term Incentive Plan.
  • These board decisions affect the distribution of sale proceeds to shareholders and the future equity incentive pool for management and employees.

StoneCo operates as a payments and financial technology provider, serving merchants that rely on digital transactions and related financial services. Corporate actions such as an extraordinary dividend can influence how investors view the relationship between asset disposals and capital returns. At the same time, expanding the Long Term Incentive Plan draws attention to how equity awards fit into overall ownership and potential dilution.

For investors watching NasdaqGS:STNE, these moves may prompt a closer look at capital allocation priorities, including how much cash is returned versus retained. The enlarged incentive pool is also relevant for evaluating potential share issuance over time and how closely management rewards align with long term business objectives.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for StoneCo by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on StoneCo.

Is StoneCo's dividend sustainable? Check out what every dividend investor needs to know in our dividend analysis.

The extraordinary cash dividend of US$2.53 per share is a direct return of proceeds from the Linx sale to shareholders, rather than an indication of a new recurring payout policy. Management has clearly described it as a one time event with no commitment regarding future dividends, so income focused investors may want to view it as a capital return linked to a specific asset disposal rather than a change in long term dividend strategy. On the other side, the approval of roughly 3.8 million additional shares for the Long Term Incentive Plan points to continued use of stock based compensation to retain and motivate key staff. That can align management with shareholder outcomes, but it also expands the potential equity overhang and is worth factoring into any view on future per share metrics and ownership dilution.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The StoneCo Narrative

  • The use of Linx sale proceeds for a one time dividend is consistent with the narrative theme of recycling capital from non core software assets into shareholder returns and higher margin financial services.
  • Issuing more shares into the Long Term Incentive Plan pulls in the opposite direction of prior share repurchases mentioned in the narrative, and could soften the impact of any future buybacks on earnings per share.
  • The narrative focuses largely on earnings, margins, and buybacks, so this specific mix of a special dividend plus incremental incentive shares may not be fully captured in expectations around capital returns and share count.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for StoneCo to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ The larger Long Term Incentive Plan pool adds to potential share dilution, which could weigh on future per share figures if not offset by buybacks or strong earnings growth.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged 1 key risk around StoneCo's financial position, including debt that is not well covered by operating cash flow, which is relevant when assessing how much cash can be distributed versus retained.
  • 🎁 Returning a portion of Linx sale proceeds via a special dividend signals that excess capital from asset disposals is being shared with investors rather than fully retained.
  • 🎁 StoneCo is flagged for 4 key rewards, including trading at a discount to an internal fair value estimate and having growing earnings, which some investors may weigh alongside the extra cash return.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it helps to track whether StoneCo follows the extraordinary dividend with any future cash returns such as further specials or buybacks, or instead leans more on reinvestment into its core payments and financial services operations. Investors may also want to monitor how quickly the extra 3.8 million incentive shares are granted and vested, and whether they coincide with clear value creation targets. Given competition from players like PagSeguro, Nu Holdings, and traditional Brazilian banks, the balance between rewarding shareholders today, funding growth, and managing dilution will be an important signal of how StoneCo is running its capital allocation playbook.

data.title: Is Pfizer (PFE) Offering Long Term Value After Recent Share Price Recovery
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Pfizer's current share price lines up with its fundamentals, this breakdown will help you focus on what the numbers actually say about value.
  • The stock last closed at US$27.22, with returns of 0.0% over 7 days, a 0.8% decline over 30 days, an 8.1% gain year to date, and 31.9% over 1 year, while the 3 year and 5 year returns are 18.1% and 9.6% declines respectively.
  • Recent headlines around Pfizer have focused on the company as a large, established pharmaceuticals player, with investors paying attention to how its pipeline, patent cycle and product mix are evolving. These themes often shape how the market weighs long term prospects against shorter term share price moves.
  • Pfizer currently has a value score of 3 out of 6, which reflects how it screens across several standard valuation checks. Next up is a look at what those approaches show and why a broader way of thinking about valuation can be even more useful by the end of this article.

Find out why Pfizer's 31.9% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Pfizer Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company could be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to a present value.

For Pfizer, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, relying on cash flow projections and a longer term fade period. The latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about US$8.5b. Analyst and extrapolated estimates suggest Free Cash Flow of around US$16.1b by 2030, with interim projections between 2026 and 2035 ranging roughly from US$15.9b to US$19.9b before discounting.

When these projected cash flows are discounted back and aggregated, the DCF model points to an estimated intrinsic value of about US$63.97 per share. Compared with the recent share price of US$27.22, this implies a discount of roughly 57.4%, indicating that the shares are pricing in a lot of caution relative to the cash flow outlook embedded in this model.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Pfizer is undervalued by 57.4%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 58 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Pfizer.

Approach 2: Pfizer Price vs Earnings (P/E)

For a profitable company like Pfizer, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand for how much investors are currently paying for each dollar of earnings. It ties directly to what you see on the income statement, which makes it easier to relate the share price to the business today.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how the market sees growth potential and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk often go with a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher uncertainty tend to justify a lower one.

Pfizer currently trades on a P/E of 19.99x. That is close to both the Pharmaceuticals industry average of 17.03x and the peer group average of 19.57x, so the market is not treating Pfizer as an obvious outlier on simple comparisons.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Pfizer is 20.90x. This is a proprietary estimate of what Pfizer’s P/E might be given its earnings profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and company specific risks. Because it folds these factors into one figure, it can be more informative than looking at peers or industry averages alone.

Comparing the Fair Ratio of 20.90x with the current P/E of 19.99x suggests the shares are modestly below that level.

Result: UNDERVALUED

P/E ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 19 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Pfizer Narrative

Earlier it was mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. Narratives on Simply Wall St let you turn your view of Pfizer into a clear story that ties together your assumptions for future revenue, earnings and margins with a Fair Value you can compare to the current share price.

In practice, a Narrative is your version of the company, built on numbers, where you connect themes like Pfizer’s post COVID transition, oncology and obesity pipeline, patent cliff risk, cost cuts and dividend policy to a financial forecast and a Fair Value estimate rather than relying only on ratios like P/E.

On the Simply Wall St Community page, Narratives are designed to be easy to use, so you can see how your view lines up against others. They update automatically when new data, news or earnings are added, helping you keep your Fair Value vs Price view current without rebuilding your model each time.

For Pfizer, one investor Narrative might lean optimistic, using assumptions similar to a Fair Value of about US$35.33 per share. A cautious Narrative might look closer to US$25.00, and comparing each to today’s price can help you decide whether the stock appears closer to fully valued or discounted under your own story.

For Pfizer however we will make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Pfizer Narratives:

Each one ties together different assumptions about the obesity pipeline, patent cycle and earnings path, so you can quickly see which version of the story feels closer to your own view.

🐂 Pfizer Bull Case

Fair value: US$29.08

Implied discount to this fair value: 6.4%

Revenue growth assumption: 2.9% decline

  • Sees long term support from an expanded late stage pipeline in oncology, rare diseases, vaccines and obesity treatments, alongside business development activity that widens the product base.
  • Assumes efficiency gains from digitalisation, AI powered R&D and automation help support margins even while headline revenue expectations are for a 2.9% annual decline.
  • Aligns with analyst consensus targets around US$28.86, with earnings projected to US$12.8b and a future P/E of 15.7x, subject to the usual execution and policy risks across pricing, regulation and patent expiries.

🐻 Pfizer Bear Case

Fair value: US$25.00

Implied premium to this fair value: 8.9%

Revenue growth assumption: 6.7% decline

  • Focuses on pressure from drug price negotiations, regulatory reforms and a sizeable patent cliff across products such as Eliquis and Ibrance, with revenue expected to decline by 6.7% per year.
  • Assumes earnings fall to US$6.6b by 2029, with only a modest margin lift, and a higher 26.2x P/E required to support the more cautious US$25.00 fair value.
  • Flags the risk that new GLP 1, oncology and other pipeline assets may not fully replace post 2028 revenue, while still acknowledging that cost programs, acquisitions and late stage trials could challenge this more pessimistic view.

data.title: HSBC Weighs Strait Of Hormuz Shock And Iran Conflict For Investors
data.description:
• HSBC Holdings (LSE:HSBA) executives, including CEO Georges Elhedery and the Global Head of Markets, have commented on the disruption at the Strait of Hormuz and the ongoing Iran conflict.
  • The bank’s leadership is assessing how supply chain interruptions are affecting global energy markets, trade flows, and market volatility.
  • HSBC is outlining how these disruptions relate to its risk management approach, client activity, and broader market conditions.

HSBC is one of the largest global banking groups, with exposure to trade finance, markets activity, and corporate banking across Asia, Europe, and the Middle East. When a key chokepoint such as the Strait of Hormuz faces disruption, the bank is closely linked to the resulting moves in energy prices, shipping routes, and hedging demand. For investors watching LSE:HSBA, these comments provide additional context on how the institution is positioned in an environment of stressed supply chains and active capital markets desks.

Management commentary on the Strait of Hormuz blockage and Iran conflict gives investors more detail on how HSBC evaluates liquidity, counterparty risk, and client demand across commodities and currencies. Readers can use these remarks as one input when considering how geopolitical shocks might influence bank operations, trading activity, and the broader financial sector’s approach to risk in the period ahead.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for HSBC Holdings by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on HSBC Holdings.

Does the team leading HSBC Holdings have what it takes? See our full breakdown of the management team's track record and compensation.

HSBC’s leadership comments on the Strait of Hormuz disruption and Iran conflict put a spotlight on how the bank thinks about risk across its global network. With CEO Georges Elhedery focusing on trade and supply chain effects, and Global Head of Markets Patrick George highlighting pressure on energy markets and demand for diversification assets like gold, the message to investors is that geopolitical shocks are now a core part of day to day decision making. For a bank that competes with peers such as Standard Chartered, JPMorgan Chase, and Citigroup in trade finance and markets, this type of visibility on risk appetite and client flows helps you judge how prepared HSBC is when volatility rises.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The HSBC Holdings Narrative

  • The focus on disrupted trade routes and energy-linked volatility ties directly to HSBC’s emphasis on cross-border trade flows and Asia and Middle East exposure, which is a central theme in the existing narrative.
  • Heightened geopolitical tension could challenge assumptions around smooth regional growth and resilient fee income from wealth and markets activities, especially where the narrative leans on steady transaction volumes.
  • The detailed commentary on liquidity, hedging demand, and diversification into assets like gold may not be fully captured in the current narrative’s emphasis on cost efficiency and digital transformation.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for HSBC Holdings to help decide what it is worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Concentration in Asia and the Middle East means prolonged geopolitical tension or trade disruption could weigh on credit quality and client activity in key markets.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged three risks for HSBC, including low bad loan coverage and a relatively high level of bad loans, which could matter more if energy and trade exposed borrowers come under stress.
  • 🎁 HSBC’s global markets and trade franchises may see increased client demand for risk management, hedging, and liquidity solutions when energy and shipping routes face disruption.
  • 🎁 Management’s willingness to speak directly about stress points can help investors assess whether the existing risk framework and capital allocation approach are aligned with a world of frequent geopolitical shocks.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, keep an eye on how HSBC describes client activity in energy, shipping, and trade finance, and whether leadership links geopolitical events to changes in risk-weighted assets or credit provisions. Any shifts in commentary around Asian and Middle Eastern exposure, or in the balance between markets income and traditional lending, will be useful signals on how the bank is adjusting its playbook relative to peers. It is also worth watching whether management ties these tensions to further investment in transaction banking and digital money rails, given recent moves like the Tokenized Deposit Service.

Approach 1: Harley-Davidson Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of a company’s future cash flows and discounts them back to today’s dollars, aiming to show what the business might be worth right now based purely on those projected cash flows.

For Harley-Davidson, the latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about $408 million. Simply Wall St uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model that blends analyst forecasts with its own extrapolations. For example, projected Free Cash Flow in 2028 is $242.1 million, and the longer term path out to 2035 is based on a mix of analyst inputs for the earlier years and estimated trends for the later years.

After discounting all those projected cash flows back to today, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $16.25 per share. Compared with the recent share price around $22.44, the DCF output suggests Harley-Davidson trades at roughly a 38.1% premium to this cash flow based estimate, which screens as expensive on this model alone.

Result: OVERVALUED

Approach 2: Harley-Davidson Price vs Earnings

P/E is a common way to value profitable companies because it links what you pay directly to the earnings they generate. In simple terms, a higher P/E usually reflects higher growth expectations or a perception of lower risk, while a lower P/E can point to lower expected growth or higher risk.

Harley-Davidson currently trades on a P/E of 7.41x. That sits well below the Auto industry average P/E of 18.80x and the peer group average of 20.23x. On simple comparisons with the sector and peers, the shares screen as relatively lowly priced against earnings.

This is more tailored than a basic peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for the company’s own profile rather than assuming all Auto names deserve the same multiple. With the current P/E at 7.41x versus a Fair Ratio of 9.34x, Harley-Davidson screens as undervalued on this metric.

It looks like Mills Locação, Serviços e Logística S.A. (BVMF:MILS3) is about to go ex-dividend in the next four days. The ex-dividend date is usually set to be two business days before the record date, which is the cut-off date on which you must be present on the company's books as a shareholder in order to receive the dividend. It is important to be aware of the ex-dividend date because any trade on the stock needs to have been settled on or before the record date. Meaning, you will need to purchase Mills Locação Serviços e Logística's shares before the 22nd of April to receive the dividend, which will be paid on the 30th of April.

The company's next dividend payment will be R$0.6617384 per share, on the back of last year when the company paid a total of R$0.46 to shareholders. Looking at the last 12 months of distributions, Mills Locação Serviços e Logística has a trailing yield of approximately 3.2% on its current stock price of R$14.28. If you buy this business for its dividend, you should have an idea of whether Mills Locação Serviços e Logística's dividend is reliable and sustainable. We need to see whether the dividend is covered by earnings and if it's growing.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Dividends are typically paid out of company income, so if a company pays out more than it earned, its dividend is usually at a higher risk of being cut. Fortunately Mills Locação Serviços e Logística's payout ratio is modest, at just 35% of profit.

View our latest analysis for Mills Locação Serviços e Logística

Click here to see the company's payout ratio, plus analyst estimates of its future dividends.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Stocks in companies that generate sustainable earnings growth often make the best dividend prospects, as it is easier to lift the dividend when earnings are rising. Investors love dividends, so if earnings fall and the dividend is reduced, expect a stock to be sold off heavily at the same time. It's encouraging to see Mills Locação Serviços e Logística has grown its earnings rapidly, up 28% a year for the past five years. Mills Locação Serviços e Logística is paying out less than half its earnings and cash flow, while simultaneously growing earnings per share at a rapid clip. This is a very favourable combination that can often lead to the dividend multiplying over the long term, if earnings grow and the company pays out a higher percentage of its earnings.

The main way most investors will assess a company's dividend prospects is by checking the historical rate of dividend growth. Mills Locação Serviços e Logística has delivered 58% dividend growth per year on average over the past five years. Both per-share earnings and dividends have both been growing rapidly in recent times, which is great to see.

To Sum It Up

Should investors buy Mills Locação Serviços e Logística for the upcoming dividend? When companies are growing rapidly and retaining a majority of the profits within the business, it's usually a sign that reinvesting earnings creates more value than paying dividends to shareholders. This is one of the most attractive investment combinations under this analysis, as it can create substantial value for investors over the long run. Overall, Mills Locação Serviços e Logística looks like a promising dividend stock in this analysis, and we think it would be worth investigating further.

While it's tempting to invest in Mills Locação Serviços e Logística for the dividends alone, you should always be mindful of the risks involved. To help with this, we've discovered 2 warning signs for Mills Locação Serviços e Logística (1 is concerning!) that you ought to be aware of before buying the shares.

data.description:

To own Skeena today, you have to believe Eskay Creek will transition from a high-potential development story into a functioning mine that eventually justifies years of losses and heavy dilution risk. The recent US$750 million senior notes issue and early repurchase of two-thirds of the gold stream are central to that bet, because they tidy up the financing picture, secure the remaining construction spend and give shareholders more direct exposure to future production. Near term, the key catalysts still hinge on hitting Eskay Creek’s construction milestones and staying within the updated US$659 million capex, but the covenant-light notes and prefunded interest reduce some funding and liquidity anxiety. At the same time, leverage and execution risk have clearly moved higher, and equity holders are now more exposed if anything slips.

data.title: GitLab Deepens Google Cloud Ties To Support AI DevSecOps Ambitions
data.description:
• GitLab expanded its Google Cloud partnership, integrating Vertex AI models into its Duo Agent platform.
  • Customers can now call Vertex AI, including Gemini models, directly from GitLab's AI tools and count usage toward existing Google Cloud commitments.
  • The update strengthens GitLab's AI driven DevSecOps offering for enterprise software development teams.

GitLab, listed as NasdaqGS:GTLB, is trading at $21.83 after a mixed stretch of share performance. The stock is up 11.0% over the past week but has seen a 39.7% decline year to date and a 49.1% decline over the past year. In that context, this deeper integration with Google Cloud represents a fresh development in its product story rather than a shift in its financial track record.

For investors watching enterprise software and AI tooling, this move adds another data point on how GitLab is positioning its platform for large customers. The combination of broader model choice, native Vertex AI access, and aligned billing with Google Cloud commitments could influence how enterprises evaluate GitLab's role in their DevSecOps and AI driven software delivery stack over time.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for GitLab by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on GitLab.

This expanded Google Cloud partnership matters because it ties GitLab’s AI tools more tightly into infrastructure many large customers already use. Letting Vertex AI and Gemini usage count toward existing Google Cloud commitments reduces friction for CIOs who want AI-powered development but do not want another separate bill. It also leans into GitLab’s pitch against platforms like Microsoft GitHub, Atlassian and JetBrains by combining model choice with governance features such as access controls, approvals and audit logging in one place. For investors, this is less about an immediate revenue swing and more about whether GitLab can keep itself in high value conversations with large enterprises that are standardizing AI workflows.

How This Fits Into The GitLab Narrative

  • The deeper Vertex AI integration directly supports the narrative that AI-driven platform improvements and partnerships can make GitLab a unified DevSecOps hub for enterprise customers.
  • At the same time, it underlines the narrative risk that execution on AI monetization and the new seat plus usage model still needs to be proven at scale.
  • The specific benefit of AI usage counting toward Google Cloud commitments is not prominent in the narrative, yet it could be an important factor in how large clients weigh GitLab against competitors.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for GitLab to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged that earnings are forecast to decline on average over the next 3 years, so heavier AI and cloud investments may not immediately translate into higher profitability.
  • ⚠️ GitLab remains unprofitable and there are questions about how well the hybrid seat plus usage pricing model will work as AI competition from GitHub, OpenAI linked tools and others intensifies.
  • 🎁 Trading at a level that Simply Wall St models view as below estimated fair value, the company already has some expectations reset while continuing to invest in AI partnerships like this one.
  • 🎁 Revenue is forecast to grow annually, and closer ties with a large cloud provider may help support that by making GitLab easier to adopt within existing enterprise budgets.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it is worth watching how often management references Vertex AI usage in customer case studies, renewal discussions and new deals, especially with larger accounts. Any commentary on how many joint Google Cloud customers are activating Duo Agent with Vertex AI, and whether that aligns with higher seat counts or additional paid tiers, will be useful context. It is also helpful to track how GitLab positions this partnership against competing AI offerings from GitHub and other developer platforms, and whether there are signs that AI agent usage is becoming a standard part of software teams’ workflows rather than a side experiment.
